questions.text: Which sentence is a Zero Conditional (stating a general truth)?

options.text: If it rains tomorrow, I will bring an umbrella.
If you heat water to 100°C, it boils.
If I were rich, I would travel the world.
If she had studied, she would have passed.

questions.explanation: Zero Conditional uses 'If + present simple, present simple' to express general truths or facts that are always true. Water always boils at 100°C — this is a scientific fact.

questions.text: The sentence 'If you mix blue and yellow, you get green.' is a correctly formed Zero Conditional.
questions.explanation: Correct! This is a perfect Zero Conditional: 'If + present simple (mix), present simple (get)'. It states a fact about colour mixing that is always true.

questions.text: Complete the Zero Conditional: 'If plants don't get sunlight, they ___ .'

options.text: will die
die
would die
had died

questions.explanation: In Zero Conditional, both clauses use present simple. Since we want to state a general truth ('plants always die without sunlight'), we use 'die', not 'will die' or 'would die'.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If you freezes water, it becomes ice.'
questions.explanation: In Zero Conditional, the verb in the 'if clause' uses present simple — base form for all subjects. 'You' takes the base form 'freeze', not 'freezes' (which is for he/she/it).

questions.text: The sentence 'If you press this button, the light turns on.' is a Zero Conditional.
questions.explanation: Correct! 'If + present simple (press), present simple (turns on)' — this describes an automatic, cause-and-effect result that always happens, making it a Zero Conditional.

questions.text: Which sentence is a correctly formed First Conditional?

options.text: If it will rain tomorrow, I stay home.
If it rains tomorrow, I will stay home.
If it rained tomorrow, I would stay home.
If it had rained, I would have stayed home.

questions.explanation: First Conditional uses 'If + present simple, will + base verb'. It describes a real, possible future situation. Option A incorrectly uses 'will rain' in the if-clause.

questions.text: The sentence 'If you will study hard, you will pass the exam.' is grammatically correct.
questions.explanation: Incorrect! In First Conditional, do NOT use 'will' in the if-clause. The correct sentence is: 'If you study hard, you will pass the exam.' — the if-clause uses present simple.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If it rain tomorrow, we will cancel the picnic.'
questions.explanation: In First Conditional, the if-clause uses present simple. The subject 'it' is third-person singular, so the verb must be 'rains' (with -s), not 'rain'.

questions.text: Complete the First Conditional: 'If she ___ her homework, the teacher will be upset.'

options.text: won't do
doesn't do
didn't do
hadn't done

questions.explanation: In the if-clause of a First Conditional, use present simple (including present simple negative: 'doesn't do'). 'Won't do' is wrong because 'will' cannot go in the if-clause.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If you heat this metal, it will expands.'
questions.explanation: After 'will', always use the base form (infinitive without 'to'). 'Expands' adds an -s which is wrong here. The correct form is 'will expand'.

questions.text: Choose the correct First Conditional sentence:

options.text: If he study more, he will pass.
If he studies more, he will pass.
If he will study more, he will pass.
If he studied more, he will pass.

questions.explanation: 'He' is third-person singular, so the present simple verb needs -s: 'studies'. 'Will study' is wrong in the if-clause. 'Studied' belongs to Second Conditional, not First.

questions.text: The sentence 'If they miss the bus, they will be late for school.' expresses a real, possible situation.
questions.explanation: Correct! This is a First Conditional. Missing the bus and being late is a real, possible future scenario. First Conditional is used for situations that could genuinely happen.

questions.text: Which sentence is a correctly formed Second Conditional?

options.text: If I am rich, I will buy a house.
If I were rich, I would buy a house.
If I am rich, I would buy a house.
If I will be rich, I would buy a house.

questions.explanation: Second Conditional uses 'If + past simple, would + base verb' to talk about imaginary or unlikely present/future situations. Note: 'were' is used for all persons (I/he/she/it were), not 'was', in formal Second Conditional.

questions.text: The sentence 'If I had a million dollars, I would travel the world.' is a Second Conditional.
questions.explanation: Correct! 'If + past simple (had), would + base verb (travel)' — this is a Second Conditional. It describes an imaginary situation (having a million dollars) and its imagined result.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If she knew the answer, she will tell us.'
questions.explanation: In Second Conditional, the main clause uses 'would + base verb', not 'will'. 'Will' is used in First Conditional (real situations). Since 'knew' shows this is imaginary, 'would tell' is correct.

questions.text: Complete the Second Conditional: 'If dogs could talk, they ___ ask for more food.'

options.text: will
would
can
do

questions.explanation: Second Conditional requires 'would' in the main clause. Dogs cannot really talk — this is an imaginary situation, so we use 'would ask', not 'will ask'. 'Would' signals unreality.

questions.text: The sentence 'If I were you, I will apologize.' is grammatically correct.
questions.explanation: Incorrect! 'If I were you' is Second Conditional, so the main clause must use 'would', not 'will'. The correct sentence is: 'If I were you, I would apologize.' This is a common error to avoid.

questions.text: Which sentence is a correctly formed Third Conditional?

options.text: If I study hard, I will pass.
If I studied hard, I would pass.
If I had studied hard, I would have passed.
If I have studied hard, I will have passed.

questions.explanation: Third Conditional uses 'If + past perfect (had + past participle), would have + past participle'. It talks about an imaginary change to a past situation and its imagined past result.

questions.text: Find and fix the error: 'If she had left earlier, she would miss the traffic.'
questions.explanation: In Third Conditional, the main clause needs 'would have + past participle'. Since the if-clause uses past perfect ('had left'), the result must also be in the past: 'would have missed', not 'would miss'.

questions.text: The sentence 'If they had taken a map, they wouldn't have gotten lost.' is a grammatically correct Third Conditional.
questions.explanation: Correct! 'If + past perfect (had taken), would + have + past participle (wouldn't have gotten)' — this is a perfect Third Conditional. It imagines a different past action and its different past result.
